purpose: Regenerated gold set: maintainer-ratified answer key over agent extraction output (template v1) for grading extraction quality and proof routing; supersedes the 2026-06-10 heuristic-sampled proposal.

verdictDefinitions.accept: claim correctly extracted and all agent labels correct
verdictDefinitions.relabel: claim correctly extracted but one or more labels corrected (correction in note)
verdictDefinitions.not-a-claim: text should not have been extracted as a behavior claim
verdictDefinitions.badly-bounded: real claim but wrong excerpt boundary, wrong merge, or duplicate (detail in note)

entries.summary: Commands emit durable packets carrying enough state for the next agent to resume work, rather than only terminal prose for a human operator.
From the Cautilus repo, npm run consumer:onboard:smoke is the shortest end-to-end adoption proof against a fresh consumer.
Dogfood and migration evidence lives in docs/maintainers/consumer-readiness.md, which serves as an evidence appendix rather than the canonical bootstrap guide.
For codex_exec, --codex-home-mode isolated keeps user config and session state out of the eval, and --codex-auth-mode inherit copies only Codex auth into the isolated home.
When no Codex auth source is available, the eval runner reports runner_auth_missing before spending the live run on cases that cannot authenticate.
When typed runners are declared, evaluate fixture selects the runner whose surfaces list contains the fixture surface key before rendering the command template.
The {runner_id} placeholder is available to runner command templates.

For dev/repo and dev/skill surfaces, a non-fixture coding-agent runtime may report the adapter-declared coding-agent-messaging proof class only when the observed packet reports matching runtime telemetry.
Doctor checks for external executable prerequisites report the resolved binary path in detail when present and the searched PATH in detail when missing.
npm run consumer:onboard:smoke provides the shortest end-to-end adoption proof in a fresh consumer repo.
In live eval, the product owns the packet boundary and status semantics while the consumer owns launch, auth, and runtime wiring, with prep and execution responsibilities kept separate.
When the adapter declares consumer_single_turn_command_template, the same command can own a product-managed multi-turn chatbot loop above the consumer-owned single-turn seam.

The multi-turn loop allocates one stable per-request workspace directory exposed via the {workspace_dir} placeholder, and runs a declared workspace_prepare_command_template once before the first turn.

When a public scenario uses simulator.kind: persona_prompt, the adapter provides simulator_persona_command_template, keeping persona prompt shaping and result semantics product-owned while backend selection stays adapter-owned.

When the adapter declares consumer_evaluator_command_template, Cautilus materializes one live_run_evaluator_input.v1 packet and expects one live_run_evaluator_result.v1 verdict under scenarioResult.evaluation.
The npm dogfood:self wrapper is the canonical maintainer-facing self-dogfood path for this repo.
The fixture dogfood variants prove the app/chat and app/prompt fixture translation, adapter runner, and evaluator packet paths.
The Codex and Claude dogfood variants run the same checked-in fixtures through their respective backends, proving the app surface runner can use more than one provider backend.
Backend dogfood variants are not eval live product-runner proof, and their summaries report productProofReady=false until an app-owned runner assessment exists for the selected surface.
Most underlying Node scripts remain directly runnable when a wrapper tool needs them without the cautilus binary.
unresolvedQuestions: Which exact script set does 'most' cover beyond the enumerated list, so the promise can be tested as stated?

entries.summary: Shipped runtime entrypoints such as discover live-targets and evaluate live are Go-owned and should be called through the CLI instead of direct node script paths.

For claim discovery and review, the binary owns deterministic packet production and state transitions while the Cautilus Agent owns orchestration, LLM-backed review, fanout, and conversation, keeping the boundary clean enough for consumer repos.
discover claims extraction-input emits a deterministic extraction-input packet with content-hashed sources, the embedded versioned template, merged hints, and bounds, without calling an LLM.
discover claims apply-extraction validates the agent extraction result, anchors every verbatim excerpt, rejects unanchored claims into the extraction audit, and composes the agent-mode proof plan.
The binary owns deterministic behavior that can be rerun without model access, including traversal, packet emission, state-path resolution, refresh planning, and packet validation.

The binary stays provider-neutral: it does not own LLM provider selection, subagent scheduling, prompts, review policy, or credentials, and does not directly call an LLM for claim discovery or review.
Curation artifacts preserve absorbed raw claim ids and fingerprints, and repos with canonical catalogs keep a machine-readable raw-to-canonical mapping artifact as compression-quality audit evidence.
The binary may validate packet shape, render summaries, and expose help, but semantic normalization, duplicate merging, and audience-aligned wording stay Cautilus Agent or reviewer work.
Discovery honors the repo's .gitignore for every path selection route, including default entries, adapter entries, explicit sources, linked Markdown, and include globs.
Identical normalized claim text declared in different files becomes one claim candidate with every declaration location preserved in sourceRefs, while semantic duplicates stay review work.
The adapter may override discovery entries, link depth, include globs, and exclude globs.
When audience hints are absent the binary applies portable path defaults for user/developer/unclear labels and understands only those three portable labels.
When the adapter omits semantic groups the binary emits General product behavior instead of a Cautilus-specific taxonomy.
The binary ships a portable default non-claim heading list, unions adapter headings into it without replacement, and reports the merged list in effectiveScanScope.nonClaimSectionHeadings.
Merged non-claim headings are applied deterministically during extraction with case-insensitive matching, dropping only lines directly under a matching heading and not nested subsections.

Adapter claim_lexicon_terms extend but never replace the built-in English claim-verb lexicon, match as case-insensitive substrings unlike token-matched defaults, and are reported in effectiveScanScope.claimLexiconTerms.
Multibyte scripts get fair treatment: length bounds count runes not bytes, and lines matched only by adapter lexicon terms route through a portable fallback instead of being silently dropped.
discover claims --adapter applies an explicit adapter file for measurement and hint dry-runs without touching the scanned repo.

Seeding classification hints is Cautilus Agent onboarding work: scan entry-document conventions, propose hint lists with before/after extraction counts, and let the maintainer ratify them into the adapter before the first broad discovery.
A classification behavior becomes an adapter-owned hint family only under three conditions, while extraction mechanics stay product-owned and never become hints.
A frozen-defaults golden test pins the portable default lexicon and non-claim headings so new hardcoded defaults surface as a test diff forcing a contract update.

The binary anchors and audits agent extraction: it keeps traversal and scan scope, verifies every quoted excerpt at its source ref, computes fingerprints, owns refresh planning, and rejects unanchored claims.

Recorded git commits plus per-source hashes bound re-extraction cost: unchanged sources and fingerprints carry forward reviewed state deterministically and only diff-changed sources return to the agent.
The maintainer-ratified gold set is the eval fixture that measures agent extraction quality against ratified labels.

state_path is the writable discovery baseline while related_state_paths are read-only orientation hints, and doctor status selects the most advanced non-stale related packet to drive summaries and branch commands.
evidence_roots are read-only roots for deterministic possible-evidence preflight that may add possibleEvidenceRefs but never mark claims satisfied.
After scope confirmation the binary records the effective scope in the packet so a future agent can reproduce or refresh the run.
The claim packet separates proof mechanism, verification readiness, evidence status, review status, and lifecycle into distinct fields with no derived compatibility label.
A human-auditable label still requires at least one concrete support item before an agent marks the claim satisfied; evidence-free text should be split or deferred instead.

Extraction routing conventions hold: broad positioning stays human-auditable and blocked until decomposed, ownership boundaries stay needs-alignment, command/packet statements prefer deterministic proof, and historical or provider-caveat statements stay blocked until promoted.
Glossary bullets that only define a code-styled label and YAML frontmatter do not become claim candidates.
Claim identity is the claimFingerprint, the sha256 of the normalized primary verbatim excerpt, while claimId is a display handle and line numbers are locators, not identity.
Review results may correct claimAudience when the source hint was too narrow, and apply-review then sets claimAudienceSource to review-result.
evidenceStatus=satisfied requires a verified or direct evidence ref with source and evidence anchors plus agent-reviewed or human-reviewed status, so weak evidence can never imply proof.
matchKind=possible can only yield evidenceStatus unknown or partial, and upgrades happen only when packet semantics are met.
Human review alone does not satisfy a claim, and apply-review rejects evidenceStatus=satisfied unless a direct or verified evidence ref supports the claim.
evidenceRefs use a minimum inspectable shape, with kind conventions distinguishing eval-summary, eval-observed, and checked-in fixture inputs.
Deterministic evidence preflight only attaches matchKind=possible candidate refs, never asserts satisfaction from filename similarity, and the Cautilus Agent owns final interpretation.
stale marks evidence whose refs or anchors changed since the last reviewed packet, and unknown marks claims the workflow did not inspect enough to judge honestly.
Agent-extracted claims arrive agent-reviewed, so the review seam serves remaining queues and review-input excludes agent-reviewed claims by default to avoid re-spending budget.
Review runs over bounded clusters and claim packets carry grouping hints that make cluster selection efficient.

The Cautilus Agent reviews clusters in priority order, subagents receive bounded clusters with excerpts and labels and return structured results, and the parent merges results keeping review provenance in the packet.
The review seam uses versioned claim_review_input.v1 and claim_review_result.v1 packets that record cluster IDs, hashes, model identity, merge decisions, and unresolved questions.
The binary resolves the claim-state path from adapter policy with .cautilus/claims/latest.json as the default, supporting either checked-in or ignored state as long as the packet is explicit.

The claim-state packet records schema version, scan scope, source inventory and graph, extraction mode and audit, refresh plan, review budgets and runs, evidence scan summary, and next recommended actions.
When --output points at an existing claim packet without --previous or --from-scratch, the binary auto-uses it as the previous packet so reviewed state cannot be silently lost by omission.

Carried direct or verified evidence refs are rechecked rather than blindly trusted: missing, changed, out-of-root, or mismatched bundle evidence marks the claim stale and records counts in carryForward.

The refresh-plan packet includes a coordinator-facing summary with status, counts, changed sources, and next actions, and states plainly that it is a plan whose saved claim map has not been updated yet.

discover claims status emits cautilus.claim_status_summary.v1 without rescanning, optionally with bounded sampleClaims, gitState, and evidenceSatisfaction, where gitState.isStale means claim-source freshness rather than raw commit equality.
The status packet includes a discoveryBoundary block that separates in-scope false negatives from out-of-scope narrative gaps.
The status packet includes actionSummary.primaryBuckets plus non-exclusive cross-cutting signals, and each bucket carries byReviewStatus and byEvidenceStatus counts.

discover claims review-input deterministically builds bounded review-input packets without an LLM, rejects stale packets unless --allow-stale-claims, and supports an --action-bucket focus recorded in reviewBudget and selectionPolicy.

discover claims apply-review merges review-result labels and evidence refs without an LLM, rejects stale packets by default, replays historical results oldest to newest by timestamps, and treats explicit empty arrays as meaningful clears.

Identity drift is handled by fingerprint: replay and carried evidence rewrite drifted display claimIds and supportsClaimIds via claimFingerprint, and fingerprint-less drifted updates are reported as dropped instead of vanishing silently.

evaluate claims plan emits claim_eval_plan.v1 packets from reviewed ready cautilus-eval claims without writing host-owned fixtures, rejects stale packets, skips satisfied claims with recorded exclusions, and keeps skipped claims auditable via planSummary.
Each eval plan carries proofRequirement fields and surface/preset-specific fixtureAuthoringGuidance so agents can author host-owned fixtures without guessing packet shape.

discover claims validate emits claim_validation_report.v1, exits non-zero on invalid shape or evidence refs without mutating claims, surfaces missing carry-forward audits, and re-audits anchoring on applied packets.
The extraction seam splits work so the binary emits a deterministic input packet, the agent does the model-backed extraction, and the binary validates and applies the result.
The binary owns sourceInventory, sourceGraph, effectiveScanScope, git commit, and per-source content hashes in every extraction mode.
The agent never assembles the proof-plan packet directly; apply-extraction composes the binary-owned skeleton with the validated agent result.
The extraction budget (source count, excerpt bounds, batching, stop reasons) is recorded in the applied packet.
The extraction template is product-owned, embedded in the binary, and rendered into the input packet with templateVersion and templateHash so every run records which template produced it.
The embedded extraction template always contains five required sections.
The adapter remains the only repo-specific knowledge channel; the template carries merged hints and never new repo-specific hardcoding.
When the agent is unsure whether text is a claim, it emits the claim as blocked with an unresolvedQuestions entry instead of silently dropping it.

templateHash is the sha256 of the canonical JSON of the rendered template block excluding the hash field, covering both template prose and merged adapter conventions, so different adapter headings produce different hashes.
The input packet's extractionTarget field is either first-extraction or refresh.
On refresh, the input packet marks changed sources extract and unchanged sources carried-forward, records a previousPacket block, and the agent re-reads only the diff.
The extraction result carries no claimId, claimFingerprint, or evidence fields; display ids and fingerprints are binary-owned and computed during apply-extraction.
The extraction result records runtime provenance (model and agent) when available, matching the review-result provenance rule.
Agent packets carry extractionMode agent, heuristic output writes extractionMode heuristic, and a packet without the field reads as heuristic everywhere it is consumed.
The applied packet carries an extractionAudit block with template provenance, anchoring policy, and bounded rejectedClaims entries with reasons, so rejection is recorded and never silent.

Result excerpts persist into the applied claim's sourceRefs with path, line, verbatim excerpt, and exactly one primary ref, while heuristic packets keep their current sourceRefs shape with no primary field.

Agent-extracted claims are applied with reviewStatus agent-reviewed, evidenceStatus unknown, empty evidenceRefs, and lifecycle new or carried-forward, leaving evidence preflight and reconciliation semantics unchanged.

For extractionMode agent packets, the extractionAudit block satisfies validate's audit-presence requirement, so a first-extraction agent packet validates without carryForward while a refreshed agent packet must carry both.

review-input default-excludes agent-reviewed claims unless their evidence is stale, keeps excluded claims auditable in skippedClaims, and human-review upgrades still flow through HITL and apply-review.
evaluate claims plan accepts agent-reviewed claims so extraction alone makes ready cautilus-eval claims eval-plannable, while existing budget gates still guard any actual evaluator run.
An excerpt is anchored when its whitespace-normalized text is a substring of the whitespace-normalized content of the source file at the claimed path.
Anchoring normalization is whitespace-only and deliberately distinct from the stronger fingerprint normalization; the implementation must not unify them.
The anchoring corpus is the raw file content including code fences and frontmatter, not the heuristic extractor's filtered block view.
The line number is a locator hint only and is not part of the anchoring predicate or claim identity.
Segmentation drift is tolerated: an excerpt that cuts sentence boundaries differently still anchors as long as the text exists verbatim in the source.
An excerpt that is empty or whitespace-only after normalization never anchors and rejects the claim with reason empty-excerpt.
maxExcerptChars counts runes, not bytes.
Command-level failures of apply-extraction (templateHash mismatch, result schema failure, stale sources without the flag) exit non-zero and write no output packet.

Claim-level rejections never fail the command; they are recorded in extractionAudit.rejectedClaims, the packet is still written, and an all-rejected application is visible through counts rather than an exit-code special case.

apply-extraction recomputes source content hashes before validating, fails by default on drifted sources reporting their paths, and --allow-stale-sources opts in with the drift recorded in extractionAudit.
The applied packet's sourceInventory always records the extraction-time content hashes so a later validate against drifted files lands on the stale-anchor finding branch instead of hard-failing.
The missing-primary rejection covers both zero and multiple primary marks, and the rejection detail names which case occurred.
Each claim carries exactly one primary excerpt as its identity anchor, and apply-extraction validates exactly-one-primary.
claimFingerprint is the sha256 of the normalized primary verbatim excerpt, unified across both extraction modes.

When two result claims share the same primary-excerpt fingerprint, the first is kept and the rest are rejected with reason duplicate-fingerprint; silent payload-dropping merges are not performed on agent results.
Existing heuristic packet fingerprints are unchanged under the unified rule because the stored excerpt equals the normalized summary by construction, with a golden test pinning the equivalence.
When the source text of a primary excerpt changes, the fingerprint changes, producing lifecycle changed behavior bounded by refresh planning.
Identical primary excerpt text in different files is one claim with multiple source refs, preserving cross-file dedupe semantics.
validate hard-fails non-anchoring excerpts when the recorded content hash matches the current source, and reports stale-anchor findings without failing when the hash differs.

On refresh, apply-extraction carries forward claims whose primary excerpt still anchors in unchanged sources, preserving labels, evidence refs, and unresolved questions by stable fingerprint, so a no-op refresh carries every claim forward.
Routing fields are part of the extraction result and routing knowledge lives in the template rather than engine keyword switches, so routing happens inside extraction.
New runtime surfaces ship with executable tests in the same slice.
The Cautilus Agent SKILL.md stays within the 180-nonempty-line disclosure budget when the agent flow is updated, with binary-owned detail kept in the binary's help and docs.
The claim-state refresh chain runs after claim-source-touching slices.
Template provenance survives the round trip: the template hash in the input packet, the result packet, and the applied packet's extractionAudit all match.

Until the deferred claims:refresh:all gate decision lands, dogfood agent packets in this repo should target a path other than the adapter's state_path because the push gate regenerates state_path heuristically.
unresolvedQuestions: This sentence sits under the Implementation Slices decision-record section and is conditional on a deferred decision; it mixes operational guidance ('should target') with a present-tense behavior statement about the push gate, so it is unclear whether it is a behavior promise or temporary roadmap guidance.

entries.summary: Pattern activation follows an explicit-scope model: review gates fire when a user request, repo artifact, handoff, AGENTS rule, or slice risk condition puts them in scope, and in-scope gates are treated as already delegated rather than run on vague agent anxiety.
When the host blocks subagent spawning, the agent reports the restriction explicitly instead of substituting a same-agent pass.
Premortem runs at two distinct points: just before the decision and again just before execution, since decision-level coverage does not cover execution-angle risks.
The number of premortem angles is scaled to slice size: one angle for a single slice, two for 2-3 slices, and 3-4 for spec renames, schema bumps, or multi-file cascades.
A single counterweight pass runs immediately after premortem to classify findings into must-fix, cheap add-on, over-worry, or valid-but-deferred, and is skipped when a one-angle result is small.
A premortem agent that genuinely plays devil's advocate can replace a separate counterweight agent, but its core assertions must be directly verified in the repo via grep and read.
Priority votes (such as follow-up promote decisions) also get a counterweight pass to catch consensus-by-accident items like tidiness-reflex promotions.
Premortem is iterative: after partially applying round-1 results, a second round runs against the new decisions created by that application.
Specs include a 'Deliberately not doing' section recording considered-and-rejected alternatives together with the rejection reasons.
Large slices deferred after premortem are recorded in the relevant contract doc as a 'Deferred Expansion' section carrying scope, why, trigger, and must-fix classification.
Follow-up numbers are renumbered when an item is deleted from a spec, with deletion, renumbering, and cross-reference updates landing in one commit so no gaps remain.

Workflow behavior prose speaks only in present tense about what is done, while rejected or not-done decisions live only in explicitly headed decision sections and goal artifacts, because change-history narration in flowing prose becomes stale claims.

User language, product domain language, doc language, and code/JSON language are aligned so the same concept carries the same name across user-facing specs, maintainer specs, CLI JSON, Cautilus Agent, test names, and helper names.
Different names are treated as different concepts, so a rename updates public prose, JSON field/meaning, tests, and maintainer mapping in one slice or is explicitly deferred.

User-facing stories are the main concern, but cross-cutting concerns such as host-owned execution, reviewable artifacts, and evidence gaps stay visible in the index and are projected into each story's proof rather than hidden.
Cross-cutting concerns are not exposed in AOP vocabulary in public docs; public docs speak in concrete contracts and evidence routes while AOP framing stays an internal design lens.
Maintainers self-check whether one decomposition axis erases other concerns, keeping cross-cutting items in the index and a maintainer-side mapping of which invariant constrains which story.

Cautilus product responsibility is written artifact-first — good binary, machine-readable packets, provenance, status, and next-work routes — and doc substrate or authoring runtime is not placed at the center of the product promise.
Good top-level spec authoring language is promoted to Charness after being refined in this repo, though which Charness skill will own the semantics is not yet fixed.
unresolvedQuestions: Is the promotion of spec-authoring language to Charness an operating promise or roadmap intent, given the doc explicitly leaves ownership between charness:narrative and charness:spec unfixed?

entries.summary: When specdown-shaped language appears, the agent first checks the find-skills inventory for support/specdown and reads its references instead of treating the work as plain Markdown or HITL.
On schema or subcommand renames, old paths must emit an actionable error pointing to the new path.

When a v1 surface name leaks archetype vocabulary, the default fix is catalog-level deprecation (alias plus silent normalization) rather than a schema bump, which is reserved for real consumer-migration-forcing reasons.

Pre-deletion parity gates are read as semantic byte-identity (byte equality after jq sort-keys and sort_by), and when a slice intentionally introduces a source-to-dest transform the parity test is redefined as compare-after-transform.
Standing gates land before slices: when planning a slice that involves a multi-file cascade, the linter comes first.

Doc HITL review includes the rendered preview: chunk reviews of public docs or docs/specs prose run docs:preview:changed, read the width snapshot, and treat an empty snapshot as a renderer seam bug rather than trusting exit 0.
When a standing gate lands, temporary enforcement escape hatches (such as Must* helpers) are re-evaluated for deletion.
Commands that write and read the same generated artifact are never parallelized; parallelization is reserved for independent reads or commands using distinct output paths.
Fixtures follow the canonical / minimal / specialized naming convention, and the starter kit input.json is a drift-tested copy of the canonical fixture rather than a new role.
Intentional file duplication follows the existing copy-plus-drift-test standing pattern that enforces byte-identity, instead of reinventing an abstraction.
Before adding an external dependency, existing repo practice is checked first, preferring to port or reuse existing patterns over introducing new dependencies or scripts.
When a document moves, every gate that asserts its path moves in the same slice, located beforehand via grep.
Go owns the shipped product surface (stable operator-facing commands with strong error contracts) while JS owns bounded helper/runtime seams under scripts/ or scripts/agent-runtime/.
The first criterion against immediate Go promotion is surface stability: while contract shape, placeholder set, or adapter indirection may still change, Go promotion is not rushed.

The second Go-promotion criterion is ownership cut: surfaces promoted to Go must have clearly product-owned behavior, and helpers layered thinly over consumer-owned wiring stay in JS until product responsibility hardens.

The third Go-promotion criterion is operator expectation: promotion pressure arises when it is natural for operators to find the feature via cautilus doctor commands or the CLI reference, while node-script seams can stay in JS.

The fourth Go-promotion criterion is binary promise cost: a seam promoted to Go takes on release, install-surface, and long-term backward-compatibility expectations, and stays a JS helper when the benefit does not yet justify that cost.
Live app discovery/invocation seams are proven first in JS and promoted to Go once an operator-facing entrypoint is agreed and the surface can be explained in user language.

After Go promotion the consumer-owned seam remains: kind:command discovery still uses the consumer probe command, the adapter's consumer_command_template still owns actual launch/runtime wiring, and the Go command only fixes the product-owned packet contract, validation, and dispatch rules.
Go promotion is judged by whether the product's promised entrypoint and result format are sufficiently clear, not by whether the consumer seam has disappeared.

Go promotion is triggered by stable adapter indirection over multiple sessions, real operator-facing entrypoint need, naturalness of cautilus subcommand documentation, value in binary-level smoke/help/discovery, and the helper being treated as shipped product capability rather than a consumer experiment seam.
Handoff bookkeeping happens once at the end of a session (just before push or with push included), not per commit, to avoid off-by-one micro-correction commits.

A fourth normalization family is not added on temptation; a new family lands only when schema, helper, CLI, contract, fixture, README, SKILL.md, scenarios.go, and starter kit all come together in one slice.
npm run lint:scenario-normalizers verifies the runtime completeness of the surviving discover scenarios normalize command.
Prototypes are isolated under internal/runtime/prototypes/ as an escape hatch and use the cautilus.<name>_prototype.v0 naming scheme.

sync-packaged-skill.mjs rewrites upward links, so packaged-tree .md file contents are not byte-identical to source and upward links in new consumer-facing docs under skills/cautilus-agent/references/ get rewritten two levels deeper in the packaged copy.
Link text must not contain paths (use filename-label or human-label instead), because the packaged-tree link rewriter would otherwise create a text/target mismatch.
Starter kit inputs are byte-identical copies of canonical scenario-proposal fixtures, and editing only one side fails scripts/starter-kit-parity.test.mjs, so both are updated together or re-copied.

Immediately after rewriting a landing surface or authoring a seed spec, a one-pass self-review with three check questions (internal contradiction, scope-vs-implementation separation, canonical-source consistency) runs before commit, and failures are fixed before committing.
